reasoning: Adopt A. All three candidates are gate-clean and, on empirical exercise (scripted full win and loss, whole-game --ascii proven .isascii() including end screens, emoji-not-ascii, five distinct growth stages, invalid/repeat consuming no water, argparse --help=0 / bad-flag=2 unswallowed, EOF + KeyboardInterrupt goodbyes at code 0, SPEC §9 order, §18 weeds-as-standalone-count), are functionally correct with zero behavioral defects — so the decision turns on contract-faithfulness and rot-resistance, which is where A wins. A is the most balanced: faithful display contracts, progress-driven five-stage growth with five distinct glyphs, the strongest named-constant discipline (labels, headers, glyph tables, prompt, and GOODBYE all defined once and imported by the tests), and positive count-based plus forced-scenario e2e assertions; its only blemishes are a cosmetic keyword-only `*` before input_fn/output_fn (every caller already passes them by keyword) and one re-typed win-body line. The decisive flaw of B is a signature contract deviation: it adds a `new_game_fn` parameter that the brief's REQUIRED `main(argv, input_fn, output_fn)` does not include, and B's own suite depends on it — added API surface that exists only to serve the tests, when injecting rng or patching select_word (as C does) needed no signature change. The decisive flaw of C is the field's heaviest tips violation: it defines no named message constants anywhere in its own source (win/loss screen lines and the goodbye are inline literals) and its tests assert re-typed substring literals despite importing the constants, the exact silent-rot shape the constants tip forbids; it also ships an under-pinned loss e2e that accepts any pool word via `any(...)`. C's clean signature and B's broad coverage do not outweigh those named-tip violations against A's balance.
